claims.claim_text: The opening 2017 defense-contractor S3 exposure anecdote, including the named bucket, exposed data types, access mode, and multi-month timeline.
claims.anchor_text: In 2017, a defense contractor accidentally exposed the personal information, security clearance details, and passwords of thousands of government employees. The data was not exfiltrated through a complex network breach. It was sitting in an Amazon S3 bucket named `defense-contractor-internal-data`. The bucket's permissions had been casually modified by a developer trying to fix a deployment script, unintentionally granting `READ` access to the `AllUsers` group—effectively making it public to the entire internet. Anyone who could guess the URL could download the files. The data remained exposed for months before a security researcher found it.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Publicly accessible S3 buckets have repeatedly exposed sensitive data when administrators or applications granted overly broad access. In practice, a single misconfigured bucket policy or ACL can turn private data into an internet-readable exposure until someone detects and fixes it.
claims.rationale: The module presents a highly specific incident narrative without a reliable attributable primary source for those exact details.

claims.claim_text: Amazon S3 is highly durable and handles trillions of objects globally.
claims.rationale: AWS's current S3 product page directly states S3 stores hundreds of exabytes and more than 500 trillion objects and emphasizes durability at scale.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table says S3 has a maximum object size of 5 TB per object.
claims.anchor_text: | **Max object size** | Limited by disk | Limited by volume | 5 TB per object |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Max object size** | Limited by disk | Limited by volume | Up to 50 TB per object |
claims.rationale: AWS raised the S3 maximum object size to 50 TB on December 2, 2025, so the 5 TB figure is now stale.

claims.claim_text: Block Public Access blocks public access attempts and, for new buckets, public access is blocked by default.
claims.rationale: AWS documents that Block Public Access settings override public policies and permissions, and new buckets do not allow public access by default.

claims.claim_text: Explicit deny wins, same-account access can be granted through IAM or bucket policy, and cross-account access requires permissions from both sides.
claims.span_hint: section: S3 Security: Layers of Defense
claims.rationale: AWS's authorization workflow docs directly describe same-account versus cross-account evaluation and explicit deny behavior.

claims.claim_text: AWS recommends disabling ACLs and using Bucket owner enforced/Object Ownership, and ACLs are disabled by default for new buckets.
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly documents Bucket owner enforced as the default and recommends keeping ACLs disabled for most modern S3 use cases.

claims.claim_text: Pre-signed URLs inherit the permissions of the creating identity and stop working if the credentials are revoked, deleted, or deactivated.
claims.span_hint: section: Pre-Signed URLs: Secure Temporary Access
claims.rationale: The S3 presigned URL guide states that presigned URLs expire when the underlying credential expires or is revoked, deleted, or deactivated.

claims.claim_text: Maximum expiration is 7 days for IAM users and 36 hours for STS temporary credentials.
claims.span_hint: section: Pre-Signed URLs: Secure Temporary Access
claims.anchor_text: - Maximum expiration: 7 days when signed by an IAM user, 36 hours when signed by STS temporary credentials (roles).
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Maximum expiration: up to 7 days when generated with the AWS CLI or SDKs using IAM user credentials; URLs signed with temporary credentials expire when those credentials expire.
claims.rationale: AWS's general S3 presigned URL documentation supports the 7-day CLI/SDK limit and credential-dependent expiration for temporary credentials, but not the module's fixed 36-hour STS rule.

claims.claim_text: The Storage Class Comparison table's minimum storage duration, minimum object size, and retrieval-time constraints for S3 classes.
claims.rationale: AWS's storage class guide directly covers minimum duration rules, 128 KB billing constraints, and retrieval characteristics for the S3 storage classes discussed.

claims.claim_text: The Storage Class Comparison table's us-east-1 storage and retrieval pricing columns.
claims.rationale: Current S3 pricing is published on AWS's official pricing page and is the correct primary source for region-specific storage and retrieval prices.

claims.claim_text: Intelligent-Tiering charges a small monitoring fee and has no retrieval fee.
claims.span_hint: section: Storage Classes and Lifecycle Rules
claims.rationale: AWS pricing explicitly documents the monitoring-and-automation charge and the lack of retrieval charges for S3 Intelligent-Tiering.

claims.claim_text: The cost example says 10 TB of logs in S3 Standard costs $235 per month.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | All in S3 Standard | See current AWS pricing for an exact monthly total |
claims.rationale: This is a time-sensitive worked-example price that is better framed as illustrative than frozen to one exact dollar figure.

claims.claim_text: The cost example says 10 TB of logs in S3 Standard-IA costs $128 per month.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | All in S3 Standard-IA | See current AWS pricing for an exact monthly total |
claims.rationale: This is a time-sensitive worked-example price that should not be locked to a single uncited monthly number.

claims.claim_text: The cost example says 10 TB of logs in Glacier Deep Archive costs $10 per month.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | All in Glacier Deep Archive | See current AWS pricing for an exact monthly total |
claims.rationale: This is a time-sensitive worked-example price and should be expressed as a live-pricing check rather than a frozen exact amount.

claims.claim_text: The cost example says lifecycle-based smart tiering costs roughly $40-80 per month.
claims.anchor_text: | Smart tiering with lifecycle (30/90/365 day transitions) | ~$40-80 depending on access |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Smart tiering with lifecycle (30/90/365 day transitions) | Depends heavily on object size, access, and request patterns |
claims.rationale: This is explicitly scenario-dependent teaching math rather than a stable published AWS price.

claims.claim_text: Lifecycle rules can reduce costs by 75-95%.
claims.anchor_text: That is a 75-95% cost reduction by using lifecycle rules intelligently.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Lifecycle rules can materially reduce storage costs when access patterns and retention windows fit colder tiers.
claims.rationale: The underlying lesson is sound, but the percentage range is scenario-specific and not a stable product fact.

claims.claim_text: S3 lifecycle transitions follow a downward waterfall model and do not support arbitrary upward moves between storage classes.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the supported lifecycle transition matrix as a waterfall model with explicit allowed transitions.

claims.claim_text: Since January 2023, all new S3 objects are encrypted by default with SSE-S3.
claims.rationale: AWS's default encryption FAQ states that starting January 5, 2023, all new object uploads are automatically encrypted with SSE-S3.

claims.claim_text: SSE-KMS is the most popular enterprise choice because every key usage is logged and it supports automatic annual key rotation.
claims.anchor_text: **SSE-KMS** is the most popular choice for enterprises because it integrates with CloudTrail (every key usage is logged) and supports automatic annual key rotation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **SSE-KMS** is a common enterprise choice because it integrates with AWS KMS and CloudTrail for auditability, and customer-managed KMS keys can use optional automatic rotation.
claims.rationale: The superlative 'most popular' is uncited, and automatic rotation for customer-managed KMS keys is optional rather than an unconditional default feature.

claims.claim_text: Versioning cannot be disabled once enabled, delete operations insert delete markers, and each stored version is billed as a full object.
claims.rationale: AWS's versioning documentation directly covers versioning-enabled versus suspended states, delete markers, and full-object billing for each version.

claims.claim_text: A 1 GB file overwritten 100 times equals 100 GB of storage.
claims.anchor_text: - You pay for **every** stored version. A 1 GB file overwritten 100 times = 100 GB of storage.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - You pay for **every** stored version, so frequent overwrites can multiply storage costs quickly.
claims.rationale: The cost principle is accurate, but the exact arithmetic example is a derived teaching number rather than a published product fact.

claims.claim_text: S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency and switched from eventual consistency in December 2020.
claims.rationale: AWS's December 1, 2020 announcement directly documents the shift to strong read-after-write consistency.

claims.claim_text: The module presents S3 Select and S3 Object Lambda as generally available in-place processing features and claims they can reduce data transfer by 80-90%.
claims.anchor_text: S3 can process data **in place** using S3 Select and S3 Object Lambda. S3 Select lets you run SQL queries directly against CSV or JSON files stored in S3 — instead of downloading a 10 GB CSV and filtering locally, you send a `SELECT * FROM s3object WHERE status = 'error'` query and S3 returns only the matching rows. This can reduce data transfer by 80-90%.
claims.suggested_rewrite: S3 has offered ways to reduce data movement by filtering or transforming data closer to storage. For example, S3 Select can return only matching rows from supported object formats, but it is no longer available to new customers; check current AWS guidance before designing around older S3 query features.
claims.rationale: As of late 2025, both S3 Select and S3 Object Lambda have new-customer availability caveats, and the 80-90% figure is not a stable generally applicable product fact.

claims.claim_text: If you store 10 million objects in S3, you can statistically expect to lose a single object once every 10,000 years.
claims.anchor_text: To put that in perspective: if you store 10 million objects in S3, you can statistically expect to lose a single object once every 10,000 years.
claims.suggested_rewrite: To put that in perspective: S3 is designed for extremely high durability, with data stored redundantly across multiple Availability Zones by default.
claims.rationale: The exact long-horizon loss example is not directly documented on the cited AWS durability pages even though the underlying durability claim is.

claims.claim_text: S3 is designed for 11 nines of durability and stores data redundantly across a minimum of 3 Availability Zones by default.
claims.rationale: AWS documentation directly states 11 nines durability and redundant storage across a minimum of three Availability Zones by default.

claims.claim_text: S3 bucket names must be globally unique across AWS accounts and regions within a partition.
claims.rationale: AWS's bucket naming rules explicitly document shared global namespace uniqueness within a partition.

claims.claim_text: Incomplete multipart uploads consume billable storage until completed or aborted, and AWS recommends lifecycle cleanup.
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly states that uploaded multipart parts are billed and recommends aborting incomplete uploads, including via lifecycle configuration.

further_reading.title: How Amazon S3 authorizes a request
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the clearest primary reference for IAM, bucket policy, ACL, and cross-account evaluation logic.

further_reading.title: Understanding and managing Amazon S3 storage classes
further_reading.why_relevant: It consolidates storage-class behavior, minimum durations, size constraints, and retrieval characteristics.

further_reading.title: Download and upload objects with presigned URLs
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers expiration behavior, credential revocation effects, and the security model behind presigned URLs.

further_reading.title: Retaining multiple versions of objects with S3 Versioning
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the primary reference for versioning states, delete markers, restore behavior, and version-storage billing.

notes: Quiz items and hands-on exercise steps were excluded per the task rules. Time-sensitive pricing examples were softened rather than forced into brittle citations. Two important current-state corrections drove rewrite dispositions: AWS raised S3's max object size to 50 TB on December 2, 2025, and S3 Select/Object Lambda now have new-customer availability caveats.
